subject: ap-chemistry
title: Intermolecular Forces & Physical Properties
stimulus: 6. Answer the following questions related to HBr(l) and HF(l).

parts.prompt:
In the following table, list all of the types of intermolecular forces present in pure samples of HBr(l) and HF(l).

| Liquid | HBr(l) | HF(l) |
|---|---|---|
| Intermolecular forces present | | |
rubric_criteria: HBr(l): London dispersion forces, dipole-dipole attractions
HF(l): London dispersion forces, dipole-dipole attractions, hydrogen bonding

parts.prompt:
The enthalpy of vaporization, $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$, for each liquid is provided in the following table.

| Liquid | HBr(l) | HF(l) |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ | $17.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | $25.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$ |

(i) Based on the types and relative strengths of intermolecular forces, explain why $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ of HF(l) is greater than that of HBr(l).

(ii) Calculate the amount of thermal energy, in kJ, required to vaporize 6.85 g of HF(l).
rubric_criteria: The intermolecular forces in HF(l) are stronger than those in HBr(l) due to hydrogen bonding attractions present in HF(l), so more energy is required to separate the molecules in HF(l).
(ii) For the correct calculated value: 1 point

parts.prompt: Based on the arrangement of electrons in the Br and F atoms, explain why the bond length in an HBr molecule is greater than that in an HF molecule.
rubric_criteria: Br has two additional occupied electron shells (n = 3 and n = 4) compared to F (n = 2).
The extra electron shells increase the distance between the H and Br nuclei, giving HBr a longer bond length.
